qa_pairs.question: What are the required steps to configure an LS-DYNA license hosted on an Ansys license server for use on the Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: To configure an LS-DYNA license hosted on an Ansys license server, you must set the environment variables `LSTC_LICENSE=ansys` and `export ANSYSLMD_LICENSE_FILE=<port>@<server>` in your Slurm scripts, replacing `<port>` with an integer and `<server>` with the hostname or IP address of your Ansys license server. Additionally, a `~/.licenses/ls-dyna.lic` file, even if empty, must exist for any `ls-dyna` module to load; this can be created by running `touch ~/.licenses/ls-dyna.lic` once from the command line or in your Slurm scripts. Please note that only module versions 12.2.1 or newer will work with Ansys license servers.

qa_pairs.question: How can users access the free SHARCNET LS-DYNA license on the Nibi cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can access the free SHARCNET Ansys license for LS-DYNA on the Nibi cluster by adding the following lines to their Slurm script: `export LSTC_LICENSE=ansys` and `export ANSYSLMD_LICENSE_FILE=1055@license1.computecanada.ca`. This license supports running both SMP (Shared Memory Parallel) and MPP (Message Passing Parallel) LS-DYNA jobs on a core and job-limited basis.

qa_pairs.question: When running a single node LS-DYNA job, how is memory allocated, and what is the function of the `LSTC_MEMORY=AUTO` option for explicit analysis?
qa_pairs.answer: For single node LS-DYNA jobs, memory allocation involves specifying a `memory` word setting in your Slurm script. The `LSTC_MEMORY=AUTO` option is suitable for explicit analyses, such as metal forming simulations, as it allows memory to be dynamically extended beyond the initial `memory` word setting by up to 25% if necessary. For implicit problems, the `memory` setting represents a maximum amount before LS-DYNA self-terminates. For example, a `memory=1500M` setting corresponds to 12GB of memory for a double precision solver (1500M words * 8 Bytes/word). It is considered ideal for implicit jobs on a single node to keep the solver memory within 75% of the total RAM reserved for the job.
